chapters.title: A monster hunter weighs what his life has cost him
chapters.text:
Wounded in combat, Geralt of Rivia spent his recovery days inside the Temple of Melitele in the city of Ellander. Sleep pulled him into dreams of the creature that had felled him near Wyzim. When he woke, the priestess Nenneke suggested a trance to glimpse the future, but he turned her down.

Two knights from the Order of the White Rose, Count Falwick and Sir Tailles, soon arrived with a royal order for him to leave town. Geralt offered to go within three days. Tailles refused the offer and challenged him to a duel. The longer Geralt thought about his line of work, the more it struck him that every monster he slew gave someone a fresh reason to want him dead. He shared that quiet revelation with the bard Dandelion. Realizing that witchers had fallen out of favor, he packed his things and prepared to depart.

If you accept that change never stops coming, you can grow even when the world pushes back.

Soldiers stopped him as he left the city. Tailles demanded the duel. The dwarf captain of the prince's guard, Dennis Cranmer, stacked the rules in Tailles's favor, but Geralt still won. The fight earned him his freedom to go. As he passed the gate, a stray vision from the trance Nenneke had proposed washed over him. He dismissed the bloody future it showed and rode out of Ellander.

This short collection walks through six tales of Geralt as he battles dark forces and tries to purge them from the realm. The stories are gripping, and a calmer voice threads through them with lessons for anyone who loves a good yarn.

chapters.title: Breaking a curse is never cheap, and the price is not always paid in gold
chapters.text:
A terrible curse had twisted a child born from a forbidden union between King Foltest and his sister Adda into a striga, a monster of terrifying size and terrible speed. The king refused to let his daughter die, whatever the cost. He warned Geralt that any hint of foul play would end badly for the witcher. The king's advisors, Segelin and Velerad, thought his priorities had grown cruel, because the striga kept claiming lives. They slipped word to Geralt about a secret plan to end the creature. Half the king's payment would be his, if he lifted the curse.

A clear head saves you from making a hard situation harder.

Lord Ostrit of Novigrad hoped the girl would stay a striga. Her reign of terror, he believed, could shake Foltest off the throne. He told Geralt that he had once loved Adda, the girl's mother, and that Foltest had stolen her. When Geralt refused his money and tried to walk away, Ostrit drew steel against him.

Geralt turned the tables by using Ostrit as bait. He watched the striga in motion and then worked his own plan, mixing careful strikes with potent magic. The curse broke, but the effort carved a deep gash across his neck. He drifted in and out of consciousness for days. Most jobs come with a wall of obstacles in the middle, yet the wall is rarely a reason to stop. Push through. You may still come out the other side.

Fairy tales hold a kernel of truth, as the author reminds us.

Andrzej Sapkowski

chapters.title: Love can wear any face, and so can a monster
chapters.text:
Geralt's road forked when he left the highway to look into a reported death. Two bodies lay in the brush, and he retraced their steps to a castle that looked long abandoned. Inside, he found Nivellen, a one-time thief whose cruel act against a temple priestess had been repaid with a beastly form. Nivellen offered him food and a warm welcome, since the witcher had not flinched at his appearance. Over supper, Nivellen told him about his past and the long hunt for a cure, then quietly admitted that he no longer wanted one. Geralt guessed that Vereena, Nivellen's new love, was the reason for the change. He warned Nivellen that his lover might be hiding a darker nature.

Love is strong enough to flip a hard moment on its head.

The same night, Geralt left the castle with the warning on his mind. Before long, his hunch came true. Vereena was a bruxa, a vampire with strong telepathic gifts and a resistance to silver. Geralt tried to fight her off, but the bruxa overpowered him. Nivellen drove a wooden pole into the creature, giving Geralt the chance to strike. Just as the blade fell, Vereena sent a telepathic confession of love to Nivellen. That moment of love broke the curse, and the man stood human again. People go to great lengths for the ones they care about, and they expect the truth in return. Concealing who you are can put your partner in harm's way, so let yourself be known.

True love has a way of forcing change, even when the change seems impossible.

chapters.title: Sometimes you must hold your nose to save the many
chapters.text:
Geralt hauled a beast he had just killed into the town of Blaviken, hoping a reward would follow. The alderman, Caldemeyn, introduced him to a mage named Stregobor. Stregobor said he had no use for the carcass, only for Geralt. In private talk, the mage confessed that he had once tried to kill a princess-turned-assassin called Renfri while hunting mutants. He called it the lesser evil. Now Renfri was hunting him, and she would not stop at anything short of his death. Stregobor begged Geralt to cut her down, but Geralt refused.

Letting go of a grudge lifts a heavy weight off your shoulders.

Geralt and Renfri met, and she tried to talk him into killing Stregobor. Geralt asked her to drop the vendetta. She said she could not forgive, but she would leave Blaviken at dawn. By morning, Geralt saw the truth. Renfri planned to attack the market festival and use the carnage to drag Stregobor into the open. Geralt would not allow that. He cut through her mercenaries and then through Renfri herself. He refused Stregobor a chance to study the body, and the townsfolk saw only a man who had just slain innocents. Stones rained down on him. A shield spell held them off until Caldemeyn called for order and ordered Geralt out of town as the Butcher of Blaviken.

Forgiveness clears the mind of anger and frees you from chains of resentment. Yet the hurt that feeds a grudge can also push a person toward payback, and payback harms both sides.

People like to build monsters in their minds, the author notes, because the monsters out there make the ones inside feel smaller.

Andrzej Sapkowski

A study by the Fetzer Institute found that 62 percent of American adults said they needed more forgiveness in their own lives.

chapters.title: The heart writes its own contract, and it does not read the small print
chapters.text:
At a party in Cintra, Queen Calanthe presented Geralt as the honorable Lord Ravix of Fourhorn. The feast honored Princess Pavetta, heir to the Cintra throne. An uninvited knight marched in and called himself the Urcheon of Erlenwald. He demanded the return of a promise that the late King Roegner had made to him under the Law of Surprise. The promise gave him Princess Pavetta as payment for saving the king's life. The Law of Surprise required Pavetta's consent before she could be claimed.

Show love through the small acts of standing by your partner.

Queen Calanthe knew the claim was honest, but she refused to hand her daughter to a stranger. The Urcheon had warned the queen that he could not remove his helmet until midnight. When the hour struck, the helmet came off and a beast's head looked out. Calanthe asked Pavetta if she would leave with the Urcheon, and Pavetta chose to go. The queen attacked. Pavetta, furious, released a wave of pure, primordial force that threatened to tear the castle apart. Geralt and the druid Mousesack worked together to calm her. Pavetta stepped toward the Urcheon, and his head began to shift into a man's face. The two revealed that the Urcheon's name was Duny and that they had been together for a year. Out of gratitude, Duny offered Geralt any reward. Geralt asked that the child Pavetta was carrying be raised as a witcher.

Be brave about the one you love, even if it draws the wrong kind of attention. And think twice before you tell people to name their own price. They may ask for something you cannot bear to give.

chapters.title: A foe in the morning can save you by nightfall
chapters.text:
In the village of Lower Posada, a farmer named Nettly and the elder Dhun asked Geralt to deal with a creature they called a deovel. They told him not to kill it, and he asked for more detail. The trail led to a meeting with an old village woman and a man named Lille. An ancient book held the answer, and Geralt learned that the creature was a Sylvan.

The next day he returned to the field and challenged the Sylvan to a game built on the rule of do no harm. A friendly scuffle broke out, and a rider charged out of nowhere, knocking Geralt cold. When he woke, he was bound face down beside the bard Dandelion, who was tied up the same way. He caught the Sylvan speaking in the Elder Speech with someone else. The Sylvan's name was Torque, and the other voice belonged to Galarr, an Aen Seidhe elf.

Courage often means putting another's life ahead of your own when the moment arrives.

Toruviel, one of the elves, mocked Dandelion and the human knack for music. She smashed his lute and turned her attention to the bound Geralt. He rolled and broke her nose, but the relief was brief. He and Dandelion were lashed to a pine tree, waiting for arrows to end them. Torque raced in to defend them, and Geralt tried to talk the elves into sparing Dandelion. The elves would not budge.

The legendary Queen of the Fields stepped out of the air. She spoke to the elves through thought and asked them to withdraw. Filavandrel left, telling Geralt that they would meet on a battlefield one day and that he hoped the witcher would not disappoint. Geralt promised to do his best.

Gather facts about an opponent before you chase after them, and accept help from an enemy when the help is offered. Just be ready for the next round.

chapters.title: Magic can be a trap, and so can a wish
chapters.text:
The bard Dandelion freed a djinn by accident while fishing, and the creature struck back hard. Geralt went looking for help and reached the gates of Rinde, only to be turned away by a rule that barred low-born people from entering town between dusk and dawn without a written pass from the king. He spent the night in the gatehouse with two elves, Errdil and Chireadan, and a half-elf called Vratimir. They told him that only Yennefer of Vengerberg still dared to practice magic under the king's ban. At dawn, he found Yennefer at Berrant's home and asked for her help with Dandelion. Yennefer agreed, and Geralt ended up watching her bathe and falling in love with her in the process.

Go the extra mile for the people in your care, even if the cost is high.

Yennefer treated Dandelion at Errdil's inn. When the work was done, she asked for her payment. After a brief chat, Geralt passed out and woke up in a cell with Chireadan, an old man, and a few thieves. Three guards came in, and one taunted Geralt and slammed him against a wall while the others held him. Geralt muttered a wish that the guard burst, and the wish came true. He and Chireadan were dragged before Neville, the town mayor. As the mayor tried to piece together the story, Dandelion tumbled through a magical portal. He asked the mayor to declare Geralt innocent. Soon the room shook. Yennefer had dragged the djinn into town and tried to seize its power, but the djinn was stronger and started tearing the town apart. Geralt ran to help, but Yennefer waved him off. He finally understood that the djinn was waiting for Geralt's last wish, since the djinn had already granted his earlier ones. He made his final wish, and the djinn vanished. Yennefer and Geralt shared a quiet, charged moment together.

Do not try to do everything alone. Asking for help is a sign of strength, not weakness.

chapters.title: A witcher faces his final reckoning
chapters.text:
The Last Wish leaves the reader with several lessons worth holding onto. Each short story bends the mind toward bigger questions about the world, monsters, humans, and the line between right and wrong. Love joined with power is a force that can build or destroy. King Foltest let love blind him when he refused to kill the striga his daughter had become, and that choice cost many lives. Vereena's dying confession of love broke the curse on Nivellen, showing that honest love can move the world. Geralt cut down men who had not yet harmed anyone, all to stop a slaughter, a reminder that grim work can be done for the sake of others. The promise King Roegner made to Duny, made without knowing that Duny wore a beast's head, shows that promises should be kept and not made in haste. Geralt's run-ins with humans and elves argue that both have a claim to the world, and sharing it is a duty, even with people you would rather not know. Bound by the djinn's last wish, Geralt ties his fate to Yennefer to pull her back from the brink, a lesson that love can drive a person to act for someone else at their own cost.

Try this. Show love through small acts: spend time together, give thoughtful gifts, listen well, and show affection in public. Learn to forgive quickly when a partner offends you, and embrace what makes them different.

Read it till the end?
